title: HTML
purpose: Create semantic, accessible document structure that works across devices and assistive technology.
outcomes: Explain HTML concepts from first principles
Choose when to use and when to avoid important HTML features
Implement, test and debug practical HTML solutions
Answer conceptual, internal-working and production interview questions

stages.title: Fundamentals
stages.objective: Build an accurate mental model of HTML before relying on frameworks or memorized answers.

topics.title: Document anatomy and metadata
topics.definition: Semantic HTML uses elements according to their meaning so browsers and assistive technology can provide correct structure and interaction behavior.
topics.whyItMatters: A visual interface alone does not communicate roles, names, relationships or keyboard behavior to every user and device.
whenToUse: Use headings and landmarks to describe document structure
Use native buttons, links, inputs and labels for interaction
Use ARIA only to fill a semantic gap that native HTML cannot cover
whenNotToUse: Clickable div elements
ARIA roles that conflict with native behavior
Placeholder text as the only form label
internalWorking: The browser parses markup into the DOM
Semantics contribute to the accessibility tree
Native controls expose keyboard, focus and state behavior
Labels create an accessible name used by assistive technology
tradeoffs: Native controls are robust but may require deliberate styling
Custom widgets offer unique behavior but carry full accessibility responsibility
Extra markup can aid structure but meaningless wrappers add noise
commonMistakes: Using a button for navigation or a link for an action
topics.practicalExample: Use a form with an associated email label, native validation and a submit button; announce server errors beside the field and move focus only when necessary.
topics.code:
<form>
  <label for="email">College email</label>
  <input id="email" name="email" type="email" autocomplete="email" required />
  <button type="submit">Continue</button>
</form>

topics.definition: Semantic HTML uses elements according to their meaning so browsers and assistive technology can provide correct structure and interaction behavior.
topics.whyItMatters: A visual interface alone does not communicate roles, names, relationships or keyboard behavior to every user and device.
whenToUse: Use headings and landmarks to describe document structure
Use native buttons, links, inputs and labels for interaction
Use ARIA only to fill a semantic gap that native HTML cannot cover
whenNotToUse: Clickable div elements
ARIA roles that conflict with native behavior
Placeholder text as the only form label
internalWorking: The browser parses markup into the DOM
Semantics contribute to the accessibility tree
Native controls expose keyboard, focus and state behavior
Labels create an accessible name used by assistive technology
tradeoffs: Native controls are robust but may require deliberate styling
Custom widgets offer unique behavior but carry full accessibility responsibility
Extra markup can aid structure but meaningless wrappers add noise
commonMistakes: Using a button for navigation or a link for an action
topics.practicalExample: Use a form with an associated email label, native validation and a submit button; announce server errors beside the field and move focus only when necessary.
topics.code:
<form>
  <label for="email">College email</label>
  <input id="email" name="email" type="email" autocomplete="email" required />
  <button type="submit">Continue</button>
</form>

Portfolio

Requirement and constraints

interviewQuestions.answer: Semantic HTML uses elements according to their meaning so browsers and assistive technology can provide correct structure and interaction behavior.

interviewQuestions.answer: A visual interface alone does not communicate roles, names, relationships or keyboard behavior to every user and device.

interviewQuestions.answer: Use headings and landmarks to describe document structure Use native buttons, links, inputs and labels for interaction Use ARIA only to fill a semantic gap that native HTML cannot cover
followUps: When would you choose a simpler alternative to Portfolio?

interviewQuestions.question: When should you avoid Portfolio?
interviewQuestions.answer: Clickable div elements ARIA roles that conflict with native behavior Placeholder text as the only form label
followUps: What simpler alternative could replace Portfolio, and what would be lost?

interviewQuestions.question: How does Portfolio work internally?
interviewQuestions.answer: The browser parses markup into the DOM Semantics contribute to the accessibility tree Native controls expose keyboard, focus and state behavior Labels create an accessible name used by assistive technology
followUps: How would you test and monitor Portfolio?

interviewQuestions.question: What are the main tradeoffs of Portfolio?
interviewQuestions.answer: Native controls are robust but may require deliberate styling Custom widgets offer unique behavior but carry full accessibility responsibility Extra markup can aid structure but meaningless wrappers add noise
followUps: Which tradeoff matters most under production load?

interviewQuestions.question: Give an interview-ready explanation of Portfolio.
interviewQuestions.answer: Semantic HTML communicates meaning, not appearance. It gives browsers and assistive technology correct roles, names and interaction behavior. I start with native elements, preserve keyboard and focus behavior, and add ARIA only when no native semantic fits.
followUps: What changes when the input or traffic for Portfolio grows ten times?
Which failure is most likely in a production HTML implementation?
